item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the late answer! I had completely missed this. Essentially, humans are not the only ones that can hear the secrets to bending space and time nor those which can distort their minds with the honey-sweet fruit of madness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really confused as to how this relates to Fifthism. I've been reading plenty of Fifthist SCPs but I'm missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you both for the really nice words! I'm sorry that the logs were hard to read and that it was somewhat vague: that was partly intentional, but it didn't quite seem to have the effect I wanted. Dr. Seruuntungalag isn't totally crazy, its a big part of the story I wanted to tell. After the contest, I'm going to work to improve this and make my message more clear: Hopefully making it more enjoyable in the process. Thank you so much for the awesome feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I generally agree. I like the blinking effect but I needed to highlight it to make it stop as it gave me a headache. The researcher should not have immediately recognized the Bridge, but wondered why the light looked the way it did and then realized what it must be after seeing it utilized. And the ending did seem a bit forced. An invasion (non aggressive), I get the researcher's justification for calling out, but assuming the wormhole seals are evil? I feel like the murder monster thing is too forced. I'll chalk that up to the savings of a crazy, anomaly effected researcher. That all in mind, definitely earned my upvote but could have been better imo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the whole "taking a real-world oddity and giving it an anomalous explanation" approach to SCPs (like the Old Man of the Lake) and this one is awesomely executed!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're similar but I had something else in mid for them. I'll post a spoilers section soon that should explain it.

item.title: Re: I graduated top of my class at the Fifthist Seals!
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for all your help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a whole, this skip reminds me a lot of the mice from Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy. Both are not from this dimension and are smarter than we think.

If these things are somehow related to the spiders from SCP-3966, God help you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's nice to see your writing character independent of co-authors (as nice as SCP-4003 is).

Thoughts
– hide block

You have a command of appropriate tone that is developed beyond your years, and you swap from a sharp clinical one to a more congenial, human one well and with good effect here. My favorite part of the article is the ability log, which dazzles with delightful physics concepts and is very clever.

I think you've achieved your goal of communicating the raw nitty-gritty of Foundation research; this offers a perspective usually taken for granted, and reminds us that any given science textbook's sentence can require a mountain of hard work (and sometimes suffering).

I was glad to see the text blinking effect justified in-article. But the bottom line about it from me is that it is very distracting. I had to highlight any given entry featuring it as well as any surrounding entry just to keep my eyes focused enough. Maybe that's a me thing. Another formatting nitpick would be that it seems strange that the the last note is center aligned.

The line where the researcher recognizes the wormhole strikes me as odd (per the article, they were up until this point theoretical, no?). Also, I feel as though the threat at the end is somewhat stuck on. I think the article would have been more effective with a bit more of a modest reach, ending at the researcher losing her arm (there's a pun in there somewhere).

Novote leaning towards upvote for now, but I will check back and see if some time or any changes push me in that direction. Good work imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept, great buildup, but something about the tone felt off. If it was intended to… fray slightly, that could work, but I don't think this is quite that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was an incredible descent into madness, well done. +1 for making seals go from cute, to weird, to cute again, then straight into existentially terrifying.
Also well done for nailing how much it would suck to be sent out in the middle of nowhere to stare at seals.

item.title: I graduated top of my class at the Fifthist Seals!
item.content:encoded: Amazing work here, like I said during critique. The feel, the science, the lore, it's all great. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My entry to the 2019 Cliche Contest! This is my first solo skip and by far the most ambitious piece of writing I've ever done. I wanted to explore how science is done in the foundation: Endless cross-tests never answer the questions at the core of the anomaly. I wanted to show how Foundation scientists would go about answering these questions, and what happens when there isn't a single clear result. I'm really proud to have created this and I couldn't have done it without the help of these awesome people:

Credits
Close

Thank you to Elogee FishTruck , RockTeethMothEyes and TSATPWTCOTTTADC for the amazing last minute crit!

Thank you to YossiLeiner and Uncle Nicolini for the formatting help!

Photo credits:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Pusa_sibirica#/media/File:Baikal-seal_4747-pho.jpg
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Pusa_sibirica#/media/File:NERPAS.JPG

Cliches are:

-Spooky scary murder monster!
-A doorway of any shape to a different dimension/timeline/world.
